million – there must be other items taken through retained earnings such as a
planned dividend.)
  Agree the movement in cash, and the forecast closing cash position to a cash
flow forecast.
(c)  Assurance reporting implications
Assumptions  over  revenue  and  profit  on  disposal  will  fundamentally  affect  the
forecast statement of profit or loss, therefore the conclusion should be modified.
If the issue is considered material and pervasive an adverse conclusion should be given
over  the  reasonableness  of  the  assumptions.  The  conclusion  will  state  that  the
assumptions do not provide a reasonable basis for the forecast.
If the issue is considered material but not pervasive a qualified conclusion should be
given  over  the  reasonableness  of  the  assumptions.  The  conclusion  will  state  that
except for the assumptions over revenue and the profit on disposal, the assumptions
provide a reasonable basis for the forecast.
The basis for conclusion section will explain the matters giving rise to the modified
conclusion and quantify the extent of the issue. This will be titled either a basis for
qualified  conclusion  or  basis  for  adverse  conclusion  depending  on  the  type  of
conclusion issued.
The  report  will  also  contain  an  unmodified  opinion  stating  that  forecast  financial
statements have been prepared on the basis of the assumptions.

3  WEDDELL
(a)  Completion matters
(1) Inventory
(i)  Sufficiency of evidence and further procedures
A  written  representation  has  been  requested  for  this  matter.  According  to
ISA 580  Written  Representations,  written  representations  can  provide
necessary audit evidence, but  they  do not provide sufficient and  appropriate
audit  evidence  on  their  own  about  any  of  the  matters  with  which  they  deal.
Written  representations  should  only  be  used  to  support  other  evidence
obtained by the auditor.
The  written  representation  is  insufficient  audit  evidence,  and  further  audit
procedures are necessary to determine whether the potentially obsolete items
are made from material that can be recycled.
Further procedures:
●
●
●
Physically  inspect  the  items  to  see  if  some  of  the  material  could  be
recycled (e.g. the covers may be coated with plastic but the pages may
not be and therefore are recyclable).
Enquire of relevant personnel such as a production manager whether the
plastic coating is unsuitable for recycling by the company.
Consider if the items could be sold to a company specialising in recycling
plastic material, in which case the items would have a realisable value.
Review any invoices raised after the year end for evidence that the items
have been sold, to determine whether a net realisable value exists.
●
(ii)  Matters to be considered
IAS 2 Inventories requires that items are measured at the lower of cost and net
realisable value. If the items cannot be sold, the net realisable value is zero and
the items should be written off unless they can be recycled for future use.
The  adjustment  to  write  off  the  inventory  would  reduce  inventory  within
current assets and increase cost of sales by $130,000, reducing profit. This is not
material individually.
(2) Legal claim
(i)  Sufficiency of evidence and further procedures
Audit  evidence  indicates  that  the  amount  is  probable  to  be  paid  based  on  a
verbal  confirmation  from  Weddell  Co’s  lawyers.  According  to  ISA  500  Audit
Evidence, audit evidence in documentary form, whether paper, electronic, or
other  medium,  is  more  reliable  than  evidence  obtained  orally.  We  should
therefore  seek  written  confirmation  from  the  lawyers  on  their  opinion  of
whether the amount is probable to be paid.
The CFO has refused our request to ask the lawyers for a written confirmation.
We  should  consider  the  integrity  of  management  and  why  authority  to  seek
written  confirmation  is  being  refused.  It  also  constitutes  a  management‐
imposed  limitation  on  scope,  which  could  have  consequences  for  the  audit
opinion  if  evidence  cannot  be  obtained  from  other  sources,  or  if  the  audit
committee  do  not  override  the  CFO  and  allow  our  request  for  written
confirmation to go ahead.
ANSWERS
Further procedures:
●
●
●
Review  correspondence  between  the  lawyers  and  Weddell  Co  for
indications  that  the  lawyers  have  stated  in  that  correspondence  their
opinion on the outcome of the legal claim.
Review board minutes for evidence that the outcome of the legal claim
has been discussed.
Discuss  the  matter  with  any  internal  legal  expert/legal  department  of
Weddell Co.
Review post year end bank statements and cash book for any settlement
paid in respect of the legal claim.
●
(ii)  Matters to be considered
A  provision  should  be  recognised  where  a  present  obligation  gives  rise  to  a
probable outflow of economic benefits that can be measured reliably, according
to IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets.
The  adjustment  to  recognise  a  provision  would  increase  provision  liabilities
within current liabilities and increase operating expenses by $125,000, reducing
profit. This is not material individually.
(3) Director’s loan
(i)  Sufficiency of evidence and further procedures
The  loan  advanced  to  Mawson  meets  the  definition  of  a  related  party
transaction, as the CFO is a member of key management personnel, and as such
is a related party of Weddell Co. This is material by nature.
The  only  evidence  obtained  is  from  the  CFO,  and  the  company’s  cash  book.
Formal  written  agreement  for  the  loan  containing  the  specific  terms  and
conditions has not been confirmed and external evidence confirming the loan
payment  has  not  been  obtained.  Therefore,  the  audit  evidence  on  file  is  not
sufficient.
Further recommended procedures:
●
●
Review board minutes to confirm authorisation for the loan was given by
the other directors.
Review the written terms of the loan to confirm an interest rate of 4%
and to review for any other terms and conditions.
Inspect Weddell Co’s bank statement to confirm the payment of the loan
to Mawson.
●
(ii)  Matters to be considered
According to IAS 24 Related Party Disclosures, disclosure is required in the notes
to the financial statements of:
–
−
−
−
the nature of the related party relationship
the amount of the transaction
the terms and conditions and whether the balance is secured
the amount outstanding at the year end.

If  this  disclosure  is  not  provided,  the  financial  statements  will  be  materially
misstated.
Tutorial note
The loan is a financial asset because it is a contractual right to receive cash. It is
correctly  recognised  and  measured  at  fair  value  in  accordance  with  IFRS  9
Financial Instruments. The matter to be considered is the lack of disclosure of
the related party transaction.

The adjustments in relation to inventory and the legal claim are individually immaterial
to  profit  and  assets,  however,  in  aggregate  the  reduction  in  profit  of  $255,000  is
material by size to the financial statements.
The related party disclosure in relation to the director’s loan is individually material as
it  is  material  by  nature.  Disclosure  must  therefore  be  made  to  avoid  a  modified
opinion.
Management  are  not  willing  to  make  any  further  adjustments  and  so  the  opinion
should be modified due to material misstatement.
The aggregate effect of the misstatements is unlikely to be considered pervasive as
the required adjustment only just exceeds the materiality threshold of $250,000 and
the missing disclosure is limited to one area therefore not fundamental to the user’s
understanding.
In these circumstances the auditor should issue a qualified audit opinion stating that
‘except for’ these matters the financial statements are fairly presented.
The Basis for Opinion paragraph should be changed to a Basis for Qualified Opinion
paragraph.  This  should  describe  and  quantify  the  financial  effects  of  the
misstatements and the impact on the disclosures.
ANSWERS
Marking guide
Marks
(a)  Completion matters
Generally  up  to  1  mark  for  each  relevant  point  of  discussion/action  or
further procedure:
(1) Inventory
(i)  Sufficiency of evidence and further procedures
–  Written representations do not provide sufficient appropriate
audit  evidence  on  their  own  but  should  be  used  to  support
other evidence
–  Evidence is not sufficient, further audit procedures necessary
to  determine  whether  the  potentially  obsolete  items  can  be
recycled
Further procedures:
–  Physically inspect the items to see if some of the material could
be recycled
–  Enquire  of  relevant  personnel  whether  the  plastic  coating  is
unsuitable for recycling by the company
–  Consider if the items could be sold to another company
–  Review invoices raised after the year end for evidence that the
items have been sold to determine net realisable value

–  Inventory  must  be  measured  at  the  lower  of  cost  and  net
realisable  value therefore  items should  be  written  off unless

(b)  Impact on the auditor’s report
Generally up to 1 mark for each point discussed:
–  Misstatements  in  relation  to  inventory  and  the  legal  claim  are
individually not material but are material in aggregate. Adjustment
of $255,000 is material by size
–  Related  party  disclosure  is  material  by  nature  therefore  must  be
adjusted to avoid a modified opinion
–  Modified opinion due to material misstatement
–  Material  but  not  pervasive  as  adjustment  only  just  exceeds
materiality threshold
–   Qualified opinion with except for wording
–   Basis  for  qualified  opinion  will  explain  and  quantify  the
misstatements

Professional marks
Analysis and evaluation
–  Balanced  assessment  of  the  information  to  determine  the
appropriate audit opinion in the circumstances
–  Appropriate  use  of  the  information  to  support  discussion,  draw
appropriate  conclusions  and  design  appropriate  responses  when
suggesting further procedures
–  Appropriate recommendations in relation to necessary actions which
reflect the stage of engagement (for example, recognising that the
further procedures must be procedures not already performed at this
stage of the audit)
Professional scepticism and judgement
–  Effective challenge and critical assessment of the conduct and extent
of  the  audit  work  and  evidence  obtained  with  appropriate
conclusions  (for  example,  recognising  whether  sufficient  and
appropriate evidence has been obtained, determining whether the
individual  matters  are  material  either  individually  or  in  aggregate,
and determining whether the financial statements are materially or
pervasively misstated to determine an appropriate opinion)
–  Appropriate  application  of  professional  judgement  to  draw
conclusions  and  make  informed  decisions  about  the  actions  which
are appropriate at the completion stage
–  Demonstration of the ability to probe for further information when
suggesting  further  procedures  for  the  completion  matters  if  the
evidence is not deemed sufficient

QUESTIONS
1  BRANSFIELD
It  is  1  July  20X5.  You  are  a  manager  in  the  audit  department  of  Dumont  &  Co,  a  firm  of
Chartered Certified Accountants. You are assigned to the audit of Bransfield Co which has a
financial year ending 30 September 20X5. Your firm has been responsible for the audit of
Bransfield Co for the last four years.
The  following  exhibits,  available  on  the  left‐hand  side  of  the  screen,  provide  information
relevant to the question:
1  Partner’s  email  –  an  email  which  you  have  received  from  Finn  Drake,  the  audit
engagement partner.
2  Business  developments  –  summary  of  business  developments  discussed  at  a  recent
meeting between the chief finance officer (CFO) and the audit engagement partner.
3  Financial  information  –  extracts  from  Bransfield  Co’s  most  recent  management
accounts and accompanying notes.
4  Notes from the engagement partner regarding climate‐related risks.
This  information  should  be  used  to  answer  the  question  requirement  within  your  chosen
response option(s).
Required:
Respond to the instructions in the email from the audit engagement partner.  (40 marks)
Note: The split of the mark allocation is shown in the partner’s email (Exhibit 1).
Professional marks will be awarded for the demonstration of skill in communication, analysis
and  evaluation,  professional  scepticism  and  judgement  and  commercial  acumen  in  your
answer.  (10 marks)
(Total: 50 marks)

QUESTIONS
Exhibit 2 – Business developments
Bransfield Co operates a chain of 95 luxury hotels. Revenue is generated from the letting of
hotel rooms, conference and meeting rooms. A 20% deposit is taken when a booking is made.
This year’s results show a return to profitability for the company, following several years of
losses. Hotel trade journals show that on average, revenue in the industry has increased by
around 20% this year. Despite improved profitability, Bransfield Co has poor liquidity, and is
currently trying to secure further long‐term finance from the bank.
Bransfield Co owned all of its hotels until January 20X5 when eight properties were sold to
Larsenn  Enterprises.  Bransfield  Co  entered  into  a  management  contract  with  Larsenn
Enterprises and is continuing to operate the eight hotels under a 15‐year agreement. Under
the  terms  of  the  management  contract,  Bransfield  Co  pays  an  annual  rental  to  Larsenn
Enterprises for the eight hotels which is recognised within operating expenses. At the end of
the contract, Bransfield Co has the option to repurchase the hotels, and it is likely that the
option will be exercised.
In April 20X5, Bransfield Co granted share options to executives and senior management of
the  company  as  a  way  of  providing  performance  related  rewards  without  impacting  cash
flow. The estimated expense of the share option scheme in the year ending 30 September
20X5 is $14 million. The expense of the scheme is being recognised in profit or loss over the
five  year  vesting  period.  It  is  currently  assumed  that  all  of  the  options  will  vest  and  the
expense is calculated on that basis.
In June 20X5, three hotels situated near a major river were severely damaged by a flood. All
of the hotels, which were constructed by Bransfield Co only two years ago, need extensive
repairs and refurbishment at an estimated cost of $15 million, which has been provided in
full and recognised within operating expenses. All of the buildings are insured for damage
caused  by  flooding.  The  repair  work  is  expected  to  commence  in  August  20X5  and  be
completed  in  December  20X5.  The  hotels  are  currently  closed  whilst  the  repairs  are
undertaken and it is expected that they will reopen in January 20X6. Management have not
performed an impairment review of these properties as they are due to be repaired within
the next six months.

3  Deferred tax asset
The deferred tax asset represents unutilised tax losses which accumulated in the loss

to be utilised against profits generated from the same trade. Bransfield Co is confident
that future taxable trading profits will be generated in order for the tax losses to be
utilised.
Exhibit 4 – Notes from the engagement partner regarding climate‐related risks
The  International  Auditing  and  Assurance  Standards  Board  (IAASB)  has  issued  an  audit
practice alert relating to the auditor’s consideration of climate‐related risks. We will need to
make sure we follow this guidance as Bransfield Co is becoming more exposed to climate‐
related risks such as flooding affecting hotels as mentioned in Exhibit 2. Other clients within
the  hospitality  industry  are  also  seeing  evidence  of  climate‐related  risks  where  they  have
hotels located in areas affected by hurricanes and bush fires which are occurring with greater
frequency. We will need to adapt our audit approach accordingly for key audit areas that are
impacted by such events.
END OF QUESTION
QUESTIONS
2  AMUNDSEN
It is 1 July 20X5. You are a manager in Davis & Co, a firm which offers a range of services to
audit  and  non‐audit  clients.  You  have  been  asked  to  consider  a  potential  engagement  to
provide an assurance report on the prospective financial information of Amundsen Co, an
existing audit client. The audit of the financial statements for the year ended 31 May 20X5
has just commenced.

and  sustainable  packaging  for  its  distribution,  which  is  a  key  selling  point  and  heavily
promoted in its advertising. The manager previously assigned to the audit of Weddell Co has
been  moved  to  another  urgent  assignment,  and  you  have  been  assigned  to  take  on  the
manager’s role for the remainder of the audit. Materiality has been set at $800,000 for assets
and liabilities and $250,000 for income and expenses.

on the basis that no further adjustments have been made to the financial statements

QUESTIONS
Exhibit 1 – Completion matters
The audit supervisor has brought the following matters to your attention:
(1)  Inventory
Audit procedures performed at the inventory count indicated that printed inventory
items with a cost of $130,000 were potentially obsolete. These items were mainly out
of date training manuals. The inventory has not been written off as it is believed that
the paper on which the items are printed can be recycled and  used again in future
printing orders. However, the items appear not to be recyclable as they are coated in
plastic. The junior who performed the audit work on inventory has requested a written
representation from management to confirm that the items can be recycled and no
further procedures relevant to these items have been performed.
(2)  Legal claim
Weddell Co is involved in a court case with a competitor, Scotia Co, which alleges that
a design used in Weddell Co’s printed material is a copy of one of Scotia Co’s designs
which are protected under copyright. The only evidence the audit team has obtained
is a verbal confirmation from Weddell Co’s lawyers that a claim of $125,000 has been
made against Weddell Co, which they believe is probable to be paid. Mawson Ross,
the chief finance officer (CFO) has not recognised a provision in Weddell Co’s financial
statements,  arguing  that  it  is  immaterial.  Mawson  refused  our  request  to  ask  the
lawyers  to  confirm  their  opinion  on  the  matter  in  writing,  saying  it  is  not  worth
bothering the lawyers again on such a trivial matter.
(3)  Director’s loan
Weddell  Co  made  a  loan  of  $6,000  to  Mawson  Ross  in  March  20X5.  The  amount  is
recognised as a current asset at fair value. The loan carries an interest rate of 4% which
we have confirmed to be the market rate for short‐term loans and we have concluded
that  the  loan  is  at  terms  equivalent  to  an  arm’s  length  transaction.  Mawson  has
provided written confirmation that the loan will be repaid by 31 October 20X5. The
only other audit work performed was to agree the cash payment to the cash book.
Details of the loan have not been separately disclosed in the financial statements.
Exhibit 2 – Update on completion matters following discussions with management
It is now 1 September 20X5 and the auditor’s report on Weddell Co’s financial statements for
the year ended 31 March 20X5 is due to be issued in the next few days.
Following discussions with management, they have stated that they are not willing to make
any  further  adjustments  to  the  financial  statements  in  relation  to  the  issues  outlined  in
Exhibit 1 and you are now considering the form and content of the auditor’s report.
END OF QUESTION

Exhibit 2
Encore Co was established ten years ago by three engineers and has become a
well-respected company in the design and manufacture of large boats used in the
commercial fishing industry.
The company is relatively small, with only two people working in the finance
department. For the last six years, the directors engaged another audit firm, Coach &
Co, as they believe that external audit provides credibility to the financial statements,
which has helped the company secure finance. The audit opinions issued by Coach &
Co have been unmodified each year. The audit firm resigned due to a conflict of
interest with another of their audit clients.
Encore Co owns two large premises in a town called Seaville, where the boats are
built. It also owns a dockyard (an area used for anchoring, repairing and maintaining
ships) where customers can rent a space to store their boats when they are not in use.
The company’s head office is also located at the dockyard, both of which are
capitalized and depreciated in accordance with their appropriate useful lives.
Encore Co typically works on 20 customer contracts at any one time. Boats are
normally delivered to the customer as soon as they are complete and have undergone
the necessary external safety inspection to receive a licence to sail, which is when the
company has completed its obligation under the contract with the customer.
4 / 19
Exhibit 3
Developments in the year
1.Payroll – outsourcing and control deficiencies
In November 20X4, the company employed a new finance director, Gavin Bee. When
reviewing the company’s internal controls over payroll, Gavin noticed several
significant deficiencies which meant that overtime payments were not always
authorized and timesheets often went missing.
Concerned about this, Gavin convinced the other directors that Encore Co should
engage a service organisation to deal with the company’s payroll. From 1 June 20X5,
Bear Payroll Services (BPS) has provided an outsourced payroll service to the
company.
2.Joint arrangement
In May 20X5, Encore Co entered into a joint arrangement with Elm Associates, a
company which designs luxury boats. The objective of the arrangement is the
development of anew range of boats to be marketed to providers in the leisure
industry. Encore Co has not contributed any cash to the joint arrangement but has
provided two of its senior boat designers to work on the project for several days.
Gavin intends to recognise $75,000 as an intangible asset in the financial statements,
on the basis that part of this amount represents the time that the two engineers will
spend on the project by the year end. The $75,000 also includes an element relating to
intellectual property which the company is investing into the project.
3.Historic boat
On 1 June 20X5, one of the company’s shareholders gifted a historic boat to the
company. The company needs to develop an accounting policy in respect of the boat,
as there isno specific IFRS® Standard dealing with this type of asset. Gavin Bee has
valued the boat at $50,000 and has recognised the boat within property, plant and
5 / 19
equipment, and included the $50,000 as income within operating profit. The boat will
be on display outside the company’s head office.

Property with a carrying amount of $108,000 and two boats which were being stored

has not yet been quantified. Gavin believes that the company’s insurance will cover

Exhibits 1 Meeting notes
Company information
Slaindar Co, a non-listed company, operates 20 coffee shops within large airport
terminals. The company is owned and managed by Aaron Slaindar and a financial
controller. Typically, Aaron takes a small salary from the company and extracts most
of his income from the business in the form of dividends.
Aaron is looking to retire and would like to extract $1 million from the company.
The company operates from leased premises and the majority of the remaining assets
are fixtures and fittings as the inventory of food and beverages have a short shelf life
and the company does not offer credit to customers.
Brand name
Aaron has suggested he extracts funds of $1 million from the company by selling to
the company the brand name of Slaindar. This would give the company the right to
trade under the Slaindar brand, which he believes has significant value. He has
decided to sell the brand name for $1 million to the company despite an external
valuation of $100,000. Due to the company’s current cash position and existing
borrowings, this would require the company to obtain further financing through a loan.
As part of the finance application there will be additional funding requested for
expansion of the business.
Business performance
The profitability of Slaindar Co has significantly reduced over the past two years as a
result of decreased travel during a recent economic downturn, with the company
making a loss in each of the two previous years. In the last two years, the company
has struggled to make payments to suppliers on time and has suffered deteriorating
relationships as a result. In the last few months, payments to creditors have been
severely delayed and as a result, Slaindar Co’s main supplier has implemented a cash
11 / 19
on delivery policy for any purchases made by the company.
Aaron is confident that revenue, profits and cash flows will return to the positive
results obtained in previous years. He has identified five sites for new coffee shops at
smaller airports and is confident he can negotiate a good deal on leases for the sites at
lower than usual rentals through his connections in the industry.
Loan application
Slaindar Co has obtained a funding offer of $1·5 million from a bank. This funding
offer is subject to submission of a satisfactory business plan accompanied by an
independent assurance report on the associated cash flows. He believes that if the
proposal demonstrates the expansion of the business, by the opening of new coffee
shops in smaller airports, the bank does not need to know the brand is his own name,
under which the company already trades. He would like for the process to be
completed as soon as possible, as he wishes to be retired by 30 September 20X5.
Audit
Aaron expressed his frustration with his current auditors that they do not have the
same confidence in the business as he does and intends to appoint Gloop & Co as
auditors of the company if the loan application is successful. In addition, the
assurance team will be entitled to free food and beverages from any of Slaindar Co’s
coffee shops for life if that is the case.

Internal control matters

1. New payroll system

for workers who are paid hourly and a separate system for employees on a fixed
salary. In September 20X4, a new payroll system was introduced which integrates and

1. Availability of information and manual journals

Q1
It is 1 July 20X5. You are a manager in the audit department of Stanley Associates, a firm of Chartered
Certified Accountants, responsible for the audit of the Hammer Group (the Group) for the year ending 30
September 20X5. The Group, which is not a listed company, operates a number of hardware stores
which supply trade customers and the public.
Your firm audits all components of the Group, including a foreign subsidiary acquired during the year.
The following exhibits, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provide information relevant to the
question:
1.Partner’s email – an email which you have received from Paula Sanders, the audit engagement
partner.
2.Group structure and activities – outline of the Group structure and activities relevant to audit planning.
3.Financial information – extracts from the projected consolidated financial statements.
4.Business developments – information relevant to audit planning.
5.Additional services – details in relation to services which Stanley Associates has been asked to
perform.
This information should be used to answer the question requirement within your chosen response
option(s).
Exhibit 1. Partner’s email
To: Audit manager
From: Paula Sanders, Group engagement partner
Subject: Hammer Group – audit planning
Date: 1 July 20X5
Hello
I met with the Group finance director, yesterday, along with representatives from the Group audit
committee. We discussed the Group’s activities during the year, and its plans to expand into further
international markets next year. They also provided me with some extracts from the projected
consolidated financial statements and a selection of key performance indicators. I have also provided you
with some background information about the Group to assist with planning the audit.
Based on the analysis which I have done on this industry, it is appropriate for overall materiality to be
based on revenue. You will need to determine and apply an appropriate materiality threshold in your
briefing notes.
Using the information below, I require you to prepare briefing notes for my use in which you:
(a) Evaluate the business risks faced by the Group.
(10 marks)
(b) Evaluate and prioritise the risks of material misstatement to be considered in planning the audit of
the Group.
(16 marks)
Note: You should refer to Exhibits 2, 3 and 4 when carrying out the requested risk evaluations.
(c) Using Exhibit 4, design the principal audit procedures to be performed in respect of the expenditure
relating to the store enhancement programme.
(7 marks)
(d) Using Exhibit 5, discuss any ethical issues arising, and recommend any actions which need to be
taken.
(7 marks)
Thank you.
Exhibit 2. Group structure and activities
The Group operates a number of large hardware stores within the domestic market. The stores supply
building materials and equipment to the public and to builders. The Hammer brand, which is internally
generated and not recognised in the consolidated statement of financial position, is key to the Group’s
success. One of the Group’s subsidiaries, Wrench Co, is a financial services company specialising in
providing savings accounts and loans to individuals.
(1) Mallet Co is a wholly owned subsidiary and operates the ‘Hammer’ stores. It generates the majority of
the Group’s income. Mallet Co owns 50 retail stores across the country. Mallet Co has been involved
in a price war with another national hardware store during the year offering significant discounts and
heavily marketing the Hammer brand in an attempt to increase market share.
(2) Ladder Co is an overseas subsidiary situated in Farland. The Group obtained a 65% shareholding in
October 20X4 for cash consideration of $82 million. The share purchase was financed partly using a
$32 million bank loan with a variable interest rate which was taken out in October 20X4 and partly
through the Group’s existing cash reserves. The bank loan has covenants attached to it in relation to
interest cover and gearing.
The purpose of the acquisition was to expand the Group’s operations into a new geographical market.
This is the first overseas investment made by the Hammer Group. Ladder Co owns 50 hardware
stores in Farland. The 35% non-controlling interest in Ladder Co is owned by the government of
Farland due to a regulatory requirement that at least that proportion of a company’s share capital is
government owned. The currency in Farland is the same as the country in which the Group is based.
Your firm was appointed as auditor of Ladder Co in October 20X4, and several members of the audit
team have travelled to Farland to become familiar with the company, its key management personnel
and its regulatory environment. Farland does not mandate the use of IFRS® Accounting Standards,
and Ladder Co’s individual financial statements are prepared using Farland’s national accounting
standards.
(3) Wrench Co is the financial services company of the Group, operating the Hammer Bank. Its
customers are mostly trade customers who obtain credit and loans to finance long-term building
projects. In the last year, the financial services regulator has been investigating many banks for
charging excessive fees to customers in relation to loans, meaning that customers can claim back
the excessive fees they have been charged. Wrench Co has received legal claims from more than
25,000 customers in respect of this, and the Group recognises a provision of $22 million in its
statement of financial position.
Exhibit 3. Financial information
Extracts from the projected consolidated financial statements, including notes, for the year ending 30
September 20X5, and comparative information are included below.
Note 1: Exceptional items are those determined by management to be non-recurring. In 20X5,
exceptional items relate solely to store enhancement costs (more detail is provided in Exhibit 4).
The exceptional items in the prior year were audited in 20X4 and no exceptions were noted.The
executive directors each earn an annual bonus which is based on annual increases in operating profit
before exceptional items.
Note 2: Inventories include materials such as painting and decorating materials, timber and doors, as
well as tools and equipment for use by professional builders and customers planning to improve their
homes (‘do it yourself’ or ‘DIY’ customers). Inventories are measured in accordance with IAS® 2
Inventories. The Group operates a stocktake once a year on 30 September in their stores.
Exhibit 4. Business developments
Store enhancement programme
In February 20X5, the Group started a programme of extending and renovating all 25 of Mallet Co’s
larger hardware stores which will become ‘destination stores’, and each will have a café or restaurant.
The programme is expected to cost $5·75 million per store. At any one time, five stores are closed while
the renovation and necessary construction work takes place and on average a store will be closed for two
months while the work is carried out. The Group considers this programme to be extremely important to
strengthening the brand and maintaining market share.
The costs relating to the store enhancements included in the statement of profit or loss, which are
projected to total $65 million by the financial year end, will be separately disclosed as an exceptional item
in the Group statement of profit or loss.
In addition to the $65 million included in the statement of profit or loss, $55 million will be capitalised
within property, plant and equipment by the financial year end, including expenditure in relation to:
– Extending car parking facilities including acquisition of land
– Constructing new facilities such as cafés
– Redecorating the stores’ exteriors and interiors
– Purchase and installation of shelving
– Labour costs, including some of the Group’s own employees
Sales promotions
In response to the actions of a major competitor, the Group introduced several sales promotions during
the year. The promotions include heavily discounted product lines and ‘buy one get one free’ offers,
mainly applied to the Group’s paint and wallpaper lines.
Exhibit 5. Additional services
The Group, while not listed, follows best practice corporate governance principles by having an effective
internal audit department which reports to an audit committee of independent non-executive directors.
(1) Investment advice: The Group is now considering further international investments in the next few
years, and the Group audit committee has asked your firm to provide advice on the selection of
target companies and the financing of any investments which take place. A fee has been proposed for
providing this service amounting to 75% of the audit fee.
(2) Consolidation adjustments: As the Group finance director does not have the expertise to complete
the conversion from Farland Accounting Standards to IFRS Accounting Standards, your firm has been
approached for assistance in this matter. They have requested that, as part of the audit, your firm
makes the necessary adjustments to apply IFRS Accounting Standards to Ladder Co5’s financial
statements, and then to consolidate the Group financial statements.

It is 1 July 20X5. You are a manager in the forensic department of Aster & Co, a firm which provides audit
and a range of other services to its clients.
You have received a request from Baker Co to perform an engagement in relation to an insurance claim
which the company is going to submit. The insurance claim is in respect of a payroll fraud which was
recently discovered at the company.
This engagement would be conducted as an agreed upon procedures engagement to quantify the loss to
be claimed.
Baker Co is not listed and is not currently an audit client of your firm. The company manufactures medical
supplies for sale to hospitals. Baker Co is wholly owned by a foreign trust, the beneficiaries of which
include the current board of directors. A trust is a legal entity that owns cash or other assets or
investments on behalf of another entity or individuals.
Baker Co is well respected in the medical sector and routinely donates 5% of annual profits to global
healthcare charities.
The following exhibit, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provides information relevant to
requirements a and b of the question:
1. Outline of fraud – details of the request from Baker Co.
This information should be used to answer the question requirements within the response

Exhibit 1. Outline of fraud
The payroll fraud was discovered following the commencement of an upgrade to the company’s
management information system. When the system was upgraded at the head office in June 20X5,
analysis performed highlighted an anomaly in the staff costs and margins for the head office. The
regional offices will receive the upgrade over the next three months.
The finance director has reviewed the payroll list for the head office and identified two names on the list
which they know are people who have left the company. Both individuals left the company in 20X3 but
have not been removed from the payroll records. These people are still being paid but the monthly
payments are being made into the same bank account, the beneficiary of which is not yet known.
Over the two years since they left, the finance director has calculated that $30,000 in total has been paid
into the unidentified bank account.
The finance director is not sure if this is the full extent of the issue or if further fraudulent activity exists
across payroll. The losses are expected to be covered by the company’s insurance policy, however, this
requires an independent firm to be used to quantify the loss for the purposes of the claim.
Baker Co wishes to submit this claim within the next three weeks so that it can be resolved prior to the
annual shareholder meeting on 1 August 20X5.

insurance claim.

Q3
It is 1 July 20X5. You are an audit manager in Cottrell & Co, a firm of Chartered Certified Accountants
responsible for the audit of Sunningdale Co, for the year ended 31 March 20X5.
The audit of the financial statements for the year ended 31 March 20X5 is nearing completion and you
are preparing for a meeting with management about misstatements identified as a result of audit
procedures in the financial statements.
The following exhibit, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provides information relevant to the
question:
1.Audit working papers – matters noted for your attention by the audit supervisor.
This information should be used to answer the question requirements within the response option
provided.
Exhibit 1. Audit working papers
Sunningdale Co is an online furniture manufacturer and retailer. The financial statements recognise
revenue of $6·5 million (20X4 - $5·9 million), profit before tax of $1·2 million (20X4 - $1·1 million) and
total assets of $18 million (20X4 - $20·5 million). Materiality has been set at $40,000.
Sunningdale Co has profit targets to meet as part of the covenants on their bank loan financing which is
for profit before tax to increase by 6% on the prior year levels. The bank loan would not be repayable
immediately in the event of a breach of the covenants, but Sunningdale Co would have two years to
rectify any breaches. Sunningdale Co has not breached any financial covenants since taking out the
loan.
The audit working papers contain the following schedule of uncorrected misstatements:
(i) Investment properties
During the year, Sunningdale Co adopted the fair value model for its investment properties for the
first time and the valuation exercise was carried out by a qualified and experienced professional
valuation expert as at 31 March 20X5. The valuation showed that all but one of the investment
properties had a value above their carrying amounts. The exception is an investment property which
has a value $25,000 below its carrying amount. The directors are refusing to adopt the fair value
model for this investment property, but have accounted for the revaluation gains for the other three
investment properties correctly in the statement of profit or loss.
(ii) Specialist manufacturing equipment
In addition to revaluing the investment properties, the management of Sunningdale Co has decided
to increase the useful life of the specialist manufacturing equipment by five years as the finance
director believes this more accurately reflects the value of the equipment over its life. The financial
controller has recalculated the carrying amounts and depreciation from the date when each item of
specialist manufacturing equipment was originally capitalised and accounted for a journal in the 20X5
financial statements for the entire difference in cumulative depreciation starting from the date of
original purchase. The change of useful life has resulted in a journal debiting accumulated
depreciation for total of $180,000 with $140,000 of this adjustment relating to changes calculated to
brought forward accumulated depreciation at 1 April 20X4. The journal has been recognised in the
statement of profit or loss for 20X5.
(iii) Gift of cars to members of director’s family
As part of the review of the depreciation policy, it was found that Sunningdale Co gifted two company
cars to the son and daughter of one of the company’s directors, David Fisher. David Fisher’s son and
daughter do not currently and have never been employed by Sunningdale Co and no payment was
requested or received for the vehicles. The carrying amount of the motor vehicles was $35,000 and
the transactions were correctly accounted for with a loss on disposal being recognised of $35,000.
No disclosure has been made for the transactions.
Requirements (25 marks)
(a) Evaluate the matters which should be discussed with management in relation to each of the
unresolved issues, and recommend the further actions necessary prior to issuing the auditor’s
report.
(16 marks)
It is now 1 September 20X5 and the auditor’s report on the financial statements for the year ended 31
March 20X5 is due to be issued in the next few days. You are considering the form and content of the
auditor’s report.
Following discussions with management and confirming that appropriate adjustments have been made to
the financial statements, you are satisfied that the investment property is now accounted for correctly.
However, management has indicated that they are not willing to make any further adjustments to the
financial statements in relation to the remaining two issues.
(b) Assuming that management does not make any further adjustments to the financial
statements, justify an appropriate audit opinion and explain the aggregate impact of both
misstatements on the auditor’s report.
Note: You should not consider the individual impact of the issues on the auditor's report.
(4 marks)
Professional marks will be awarded for the demonstration of skill in analysis and evaluation, and
professional scepticism and judgement in your answer.
(5 marks)

AAA Pre-December 2024 PP Mock - Answers
Calibri Co - Suggested Solution:
Briefing Notes
To: Gill Sans, Calibri Co audit engagement partner
From: Audit Manager
Subject: Audit planning in relation to the Calibri Co
Introduction
These briefing notes have been prepared to assist in planning the audit of Calibri Co
for the year ending 30 September 20X5. The notes begin with an evaluation of the
business risks facing the client followed by an evaluation and prioritisation of the risks
of material misstatement which should be considered in planning the Calibri Co audit.
The notes also include justification why inventories of parts and used cars have been
identified as risks of material misstatement along with the recommended principal
audit procedures which have been designed in respect of the valuation of inventory of
used cars. Finally, the notes discuss the ethical and professional issues arising in
relation to the proposed sustainability reporting to be published within the annual
report.
(a) Evaluation of business risks
Corporate governance
Calibri Co has a weak governance structure.  Until recently, the company did not have
any non-executive directors or an internal audit department who bring expertise to the
board and act as oversight of the executive directors and operating of the company.
Whilst the company is not currently listed it is still best practice to follow guidelines on
corporate governance.
The recent recruitment of two non-executive directors and the creation of the internal
audit department demonstrates the company’s willingness to rectify this, however the
areas of expertise and number of non -executive directors is inadequate.  Both new
directors are experienced in the car industry but there is a lack of financial or other
business expertise represented.  These weaknesses leave the company exposed to
a lack of challenge at board level and potential for suboptimal decision making by the
executive directors.
Internal audit
The new internal audit department is also lacking in experience and the appointment
of a newly qualified accountant rather than someone with experience in internal audit
could also reduce the effectiveness of the department. This could lead to inefficiencies
in controls and expose the company to loss due to fraud.
Potential listing
The company is hoping to attain a stock exchange listing in the near future.  At present
the governance structure of the company is not in line with best practice for listed
companies and the company may be unable to attract sufficient levels of investors to
enable this to be a success.  It is also the case that compliance costs will increase
should the company obtain a listing which may increase cost, reducing profitability
without any corresponding benefit of access to finance.
Loss of franchise rights
Calibri Co is highly dependent on Perpetua Cars  o for all its new car sales and
associated Perpetua Cars  branded parts.  If the rights are lost, for example, through
failure to achieve satisfactory KPIs, or if any disruption occurs in Perpetua Cars Co’s
operations then Calibri Co would lose their main revenue stream and may not be able
to continue as a going concern. This is a particularly high risk given that Calibri Co has
lost a franchise location in the year.
This will also have an impact on Calibri Co being able to trade independently if
franchises are lost as they will lose the premises (as they are leased from Perpetua
Cars ) that they trade in.
Sales volumes - market reliance re new cars
New cars represent 70% of Calibri Co’s projected revenue and as such are highly
significant.  The market for new cars is impacted by economic cycles, with people less
likely to purchase a new car during periods of economic downturn.  In addition,
environmental concerns are likely to resul t in customers using their existing cars for
longer periods of time or replacing older carbon fuel reliant cars with more
environmentally sustainable electric cars.  Both of these could have significant impacts
on Calibri Co revenue levels and profits as a result.
Obsolete inventory
Whilst Calibri Co is not exposed to risk in respect of new cars on site, they do hold
large levels of second-hand car and parts inventory.  Inventory of parts has increased
by 21% during the year and second-hand cars have increased by 30%. This is not in
line with the projected revenue growth for the year of 4%. There are risks arising from
the growth in inventory holdings.  There may be significant costs involved in holding
inventory in terms of working capital to be financed and the space required to stor e
the inventory.  This is likely to increase operating costs and, depending on how the
working capital has been financed, finance costs, both of which will reduce profit for
the year.  In addition, some of this inventory is likely to be either obsolete or otherwise
unsaleable which would lead to inventory write downs, further decreasing profit. This
risk is heightened by the indications of old inventory of parts.
Electric vehicles
Calibri Co is only able to sell new cars made by Perpetua Cars Co which currently
does not have an electric model (the first one is expected in three years’ time). As
customers environmental awareness rises, and fuel costs rise, customers may seek
out electric vehicles. There is a business risk that custom will be lost by Calibri Co as
it does not offer the range of vehicles which competitors do offer.
(b) Evaluation and prioritisation of significant risks of material misstatement
Materiality
For the purposes of these briefing notes the following overall materiality level will be
used to assess the significance of identified risks and as requested this has been
based on the profitability of the Calibri Co.
Benchmarks
5 – 10% of profit before tax gives a range of $1,000,000 – $1,950,000
As the company is planning for a stock exchange listing there is significant potential
pressure on the financial results for the year where management estimates and
judgements may be made in a more favourable way. Therefore, the company has
been considered to be a high audit risk and as such the lower benchmark of $1 million
will be used for preliminary materiality.
(Tutorial note, materiality calculations before rounding will also be awarded credit)
Pressure on results
The company faces pressure on results in two areas, firstly the need to achieve KPIs
required by Perpetua Cars  Co to retain the use of the franchising arrangement and
secondly as a result of the proposed stock market listing next year.  The finance
director has already stressed the importance of the need to show strong results for the
year ahead of the listing. This may result in the failure to recognise all required costs
such as impairment expenses discussed below or causing the board to make
judgements that are overly optimistic, for example, when assessing obsolete
inventory.
There is additional pressure with respect to the KPIs set by Perpetua Cars  Co
especially due to the withdrawal of the franchise rights at one of the eight showroom
locations.  These issues combined make the risk of management bias an overriding
risk.  Specific examples of potential bias are discussed further in the analysis below.
Corporate governance and controls
In addition to the high risk of bias, the company lacks oversight of accounting decisions
and controls due to the lack of an independent audit committee and the absence of a
non-executive director with recent relevant financial experience.
In addition, the new internal audit department has not been functioning for the whole
period and appears to lack expertise and resource.  As such we cannot rely on internal
audit when assessing the controls at the company and the risk of oversight.  This
means that control risk will be assessed as high in areas of management estimation
or judgement.
Impairment of the assets arising as a result of loss of franchise
The withdrawal of the franchise rights at one of the company’s locations is an indicator
of impairment of the assets associated with the cash generating unit (CGU).  IAS® 36
Impairment of Assets states that assets should be assessed for indicators of
impairment each reporting period and an impairment test performed where there is an
indication of impairment.  As such an impairment review is required and this has not
been performed.
While most of the assets associated with the site are individually immaterial, the total
value of the assets including the franchise fee associated with the CGU of $1.2 million
is material.   An asset is impaired where the carrying amount of the asset is higher
than the recoverable amount.  The recoverable amount is the higher of the fair value
less costs of disposal and the value in use of the asset.  Where the assets in a CGU
are impaired, the impairment is first written off against any obviously impaired  assets
(to no lower than their individual recoverable amount), then pro -rated ac ross the
remaining assets of the CGU.
The CGU should therefore be assessed to determine the appropriate recoverable
amount of the assets.  It is likely that the franchise fee will have to be fully written off.
The fixtures and fittings value will depend on the extent to which they can be util ised
in other showrooms but it is likely that at least some will be surplus to requirements
and have little sales value as they are specific to the Perpetua Cars brand.  This is an
area where management judgement will be required and may be subject to over
optimistic assessment.
The right-of-use lease asset that will be held as a non -current asset for the CGU will
also have to be assessed for impairment and written down appropriately.  The
associated changes to cash flows relating to the lease will also impact upon the lease
liability.  This is a non -routine transaction and is therefore subject to a greater risk of
error.
Should the impairment and the associated costs to exit the franchise not be
appropriately recognised then assets will be overstated and expenses understated.
Intangible asset
The total value of the franchise fee asset for the remaining seven franchised sites is
$5.6 million (7 x $800,000) which is material.
Currently the company hold the franchise fees for each franchise site as an intangible
asset with an indefinite life.  Whilst the asset does meet the criteria to be recognised
as an intangible asset, the assessment that the franchise fees have an indefinite life
may not be appropriate.
Where an intangible asset is deemed to have an indefinite life, it should not be
amortised. However, its useful life should be reviewed each reporting period to
determine whether events and circumstances continue to support an indefinite useful
life assess ment for that asset. The asset should also be assessed for impairment
annually.
There does not appear to have been an impairment review of the franchise fees nor
an assessment whether the indefinite life is still justified.  Given the loss of one
franchise site, it is possible that others may be withdrawn or not renewed.  As such, a
review of each franchise site and performance against KPIs will be needed by Calibri
Co to demonstrate whether the intangible assets are at risk of impairment and an
assessment of whether the indefinite life is appropriate will also have to be performed.
As this is an area requiring management judgement, this is a significant risk and the
failure to amortise or impair the licences if needed would result in overstated assets
and understated expenses.
Penalty for early loss of franchise
Perpetua Cars Co has notified Calibri Co regarding the withdrawal of the franchise,
and according to IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets , a
provision should be recognised if there is a present obligation as a result of a past
event, and payment is probable for which a reliable estimate can be made. There is a
present obligation as a result of past events namely the failure of Calibri Co to maintain
their KPIs and the notification by Perpetua Cars Co to withdraw the franchise. The
payment is probable and a reliable estimate can be made of the liability ($350,000 is
the agreed figure). However, this is an immaterial balance, but given that it is part of
the wider issue regarding the Perpetua Cars Co franchise, the audit team should be
aware of any potential disclosure or provisions.
Conclusion
Control risk will be assessed as high on this audit due to the weak controls and
governance combined with the potential for bias and management arising from the
desire to obtain a stock market listing.    This is a significant area that should be
considered when planning the audit of Calibri Co, as there will be the requirement for
additional substantive testing, which will take more time and resource. In terms of
material balances, the carrying amount of the intangible assets of the franchise fee will
need careful scrutiny, given its value ($5.6million) and the requirement for
management judgement to assess whether it has become impaired. In addition, the
risk of f urther loss of franchises creates a significant bias risk in reporting the KPIs
used by Perpetua Cars Co to assess the performance of Calibri Co.
(c) (i)Justify why the inventories of parts and used cars have been identified
as significant risks of material misstatement
Inventory roll forward
Calibri Co conduct a full inventory count in March each year and the year-end figure
is obtained using a roll-forward of the transactions which occur between the date of
the count and the year end. This means that in order to determine the inventory
figures at 30 September 20X5 the company will be required to roll-forward
transactions for a period of six months. This is a significant period of time, during
which a large number of transactions will take place not only in relation to car sales
but also the use of parts during services and repairs. This approach to inventory
counting increases the level of control risk as the company will need to have strong
controls in place to ensure these reconciliations can be performed accurately. If the
controls are not operating effectively there is a significant risk that inventory might be
incomplete or overstated.
Parts inventory - long holding period
The inventory of parts is not material and this has increased by 21% during the year
and this includes $80,000 of inventory held at the site where the franchise licence has
been withdrawn.  In addition, it was noted at the inventory count some of the inventory
held had been marked with five different coloured stickers suggesting that it is at least
five years old.  This could indicate that some of the parts inventory are obsolete.
IAS 2 Inventories, requires inventory to be held at the lower of cost and net realisable
value (NRV).  If inventory is obsolete it may be the case that the NRV of the inventory
is lower than cost and a write off or allowance for obsolete inventory is required.
Failure to write down the value of inventory would result in an overstatement of current
assets and an understatement of cost of sales. This is an area of judgement and one
where management bias would result in overly optimistic assessment of the NRV  of
the assets.
Used cars inventory – judgemental
Used car inventory levels have increased by 30%.  In addition, some used car
inventory was identified by the audit team as unsaleable during the inventory count
which is an indicator that used car inventory may be overvalued.
The valuation of used cars is a highly judgemental assessment with significant
estimation uncertainty as the selling price of these vehicles may be difficult to
determine and will be affected by a number of parameters.  While there are likely to
be many similar cars available for comparison, the determination of the condition and
specification of each car will impact its selling price.  The selling price which can  be
achieved for each used car will also be impacted by market conditions and local levels
of demand.  This means that judgement will be required by suitably experienced
management to value used inventory.
The specific and judgemental nature of these valuations also provide an opportunity
for management bias and as such, is of high inherent risk.  Should the valuations be
overly optimistic then inventory will be overstated and profits overstated as a result.
Conclusion
Due to the material nature of the asset on the statement of financial position, and it’s
impact on the profitability of the company, this is a significant risk of material
misstatement. Experts will be required to assess the condition of these used vehicles
as it would not necessarily be o bvious to a non -expert whether a used vehicle is
saleable in its current condition. As management judgement is involved in the
valuation of the used vehicle inventory as well, this is an area where manipulation of
the financial statements may occur. Therefore, the risk assessed in this area of the
audit is high.
(c)(ii)Design the principal audit procedures to be performed on the valuation of
the used car inventory
• Discuss the process for valuation of used cars with management and identify
controls in place over the initial recorded cost of the vehicles accepted as
trade in and the ongoing valuations of used vehicles.
• For a sample of used cars, trace the initial cost recorded to the part exchange
allowance recorded on the associated invoice for the related new car sale to
confirm the recorded cost.
• Where repairs are required to used cars obtained prior to sale, ensure the
additional costs incurred to get the cars to a saleable condition have been
included in the cost recorded in the inventory listings.
• Perform analytical review of used car inventory, comparing volumes held and
average car value with historical and market data to determine any
unexpected variations.
• Assess Calibri Co’s methodology for calculating inventory allowance with
respect to used vehicles and ensure this is in line with auditor’s understanding
of the industry through benchmarking against market data
• Use sensitivity analysis to assess the susceptibility of the valuations to
changes in market conditions
• For a sample of used cars sold during the year compare sale price to
inventory valuations to assess the reliability of managements estimations of
values for individual cars
• For a sample of inventory held at the year end, review post year-end sales
prices to ascertain that this were in excess of cost/year-end valuation
• Compare values of specific inventory items at the year end with similar
specifical and conditions via online car sale websites in the same locations
• Reconcile the inventory roll forward from the results of the inventory count
attendance to the year-end balance following up on the subsequent events
with respect to  any obviously damage inventory and ensure appropriate year-
end allowances have been made for used car inventory
• Review aged inventory listing to identify any cars which have been held for
more than one year and discuss the valuation of these cars with sales
managers to identify if appropriate allowances have been made in respect of
these cars.
(d) Ethical and professional issues
The request by Calibri Co to help determine and calculate appropriate sustainability
KPIs is a request to perform non-assurance services to an audit client. The IESBA
International Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants (the Code) does not
specifically cover this type of engagement so the audit firm should apply the
general framework to consider whether it is appropriate to provide the service. This
means that the firm should evaluate the significance of the threats to objectivity
and consider whether safeguards can reduce the threats to an acceptable level.
Firstly, the firm should also consider whether performing the service would mean
performing a management responsibility, which is also a potential threat to auditor
independence, as establishment and calculation of such KPIs is the remit of
management. Assuming management responsibility is prohibited by the Code.
It may be possible to safeguard this threat by ensuring that management make all
judgements and decisions that are the responsibility of management for example,
by ensuring that client management designate and individual with suitable skill,
knowledge and experience to oversee the services and be responsible for all
decisions.
A self-review threat could arise if Tahoma Associates provides the service to Calibri
Co. Some of the social and environmental information could be related to
transactions or balances within the financial statements which will be subject to
audit, for example the value of charitable donations, analysis of payroll (eg gender
pay gap analysi s), expenses relating to waste, use of water, emissions etc. The
self-review threat means that less scrutiny may be used in performing procedures
due to over-reliance on work previously performed by the audit firm. This potentially
impacts on the level of professional scepticism applied during the audit and the
quality of work carried out.
There could be a further self-review threat depending on whether the sustainability
information will form part of Calibri Co’s annual report. If this is the case, the audit
team is required by ISA 720 The Auditor’s Responsibilities Relating to Other
Information, to read the other information included in the annual report and to
consider whether there is a material inconsistency between the other information
and the financial statements. . This requirement creates a self -review threat if
members of the audit  team have been involved with the additional service to
provide advice on measurement of the sustainability information
If Calibri Co obtains a stock exchange listing then it would be prohibited to perform
services which involve deriving any amounts which may have a material impact on
the financial statements.  Whilst Calibri Co is not listed or if the KPIs are determined
not to have a material impact on the financial statements then Tahoma Associates
would be able to provide the services if sufficient safeguards are in place, such as
a separate team from those providing the audit and an independent quality review
of both assignments.
With respect to the request to provide assurance on the KPIs, it would not be
possible to provide assurance on KPIs calculated by the audit firm as the self -
review is likely to be considered too significant for safeguards to mitigate.  It is
possible however that Tahoma Associates could provide assurance on KPIs which
have been calculated by management.  This would be under a separate
engagement letter from the audit.
Before deciding whether it is appropriate to provide either of these services, the
firm should consider whether it has the skills and competencies to perform the
investigation. This can be specialised work and it is not necessarily the case that
the firm will have staff with the appropriate skills available to carry out the work.
Overall, whilst the client is not listed it may be possible to provide one but not both
of the services requested however it is likely that the assurance assignment will
create fewer threats to objectivity.  It should be noted, however, that should the
firm assist in the preparation and calculation of the KPIs in the current year, these
figures could form part of the comparative information and opening balances
forming part of the subsequent financial statements and if the company is listed at
that point, then Tahoma Associates would not be permitted to perform an audit on
these figures.
Tutorial note: Credit will be allowed where candidates discuss the problems in
providing assurance on sustainability KPIs in the context of the scenario (not generic
points)
