Question 3 – Cuckoo ......................................................... 17
Conclusion ......................................................................... 22
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  2
General comments
This examiner’s report should be used in conjunction with the published March/June
2024 exam which can be found on the ACCA Practice Platform.
In this report, the examining team provide constructive guidance on how to answer
the questions while sharing their observations from the marking process, highlighting
the strengths and weaknesses of candidates who attempted these questions. Future
candidates can use this examiner’s report as part of their exam preparation,
attempting question practice on the ACCA Practice Platform and reviewing the
published answers alongside this report.
Format of exam
The Advanced Audit and Assurance (AAA) examination comprises two sections.
Section A contains one compulsory question for 50 marks in total. Section B has two
compulsory questions for 25 marks each. There were 10 professional skills marks
were available within Section A for communication, analysis and evaluation (A&E),
scepticism and commercial acumen. In Section B there are 5 professional skills
marks in each question from at least two of analysis and evaluation, scepticism and
commercial acumen. The remaining 80 technical marks were available for applying
appropriate technical knowledge in response to the requirements.
Section A

Question 1 – Magenta
It is 1 July 20X5.  You are a manager in the audit department of Plum & Co, a firm
of Chartered Certified Accountants, responsible for the audit of the Magenta
Group, a listed entity, which has a financial year ending 30 September 20X5. The
Group operates in the oil and gas sector.
Plum & Co is responsible for the audit of the Group consolidated financial
statements and the individual financial statements of all the Group companies.
Format of the question
This question was a typical Section A question set at the planning stage of the audit
of the Magenta Group (the Group), with requirements focusing on an evaluation of
the significant business risks faced by the Group, justification as to why certain
issues had been identified as significant risks of material misstatements, specific
audit procedures in relation to the impairment of the cash generating units for the
extraction of oil and gas and a discussion of issues arising from an email received
from the audit committee in relation to the Group’s compliance with laws and
regulations. The Section A question is typically where candidates perform best and
there have been more focused answers in recent sessions. It is pleasing to see that
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  3
many candidates appeared to have taken note of the guidance provided by the
examining team in this area.
The Group in the scenario was a large, listed entity and an existing client of the firm,
operating in the oil and gas industry.
Candidates should note that they are not expected to have detailed industry specific
knowledge when answering questions in this examination and the scenario will
always have enough information to enable sufficient specific risks to be identified
and evaluated to achieve full marks.
Several exhibits were provided to candidates to enable them to develop an
understanding of the specific issues relevant to the audit. These were as follows:
1. Partner’s email - an email you have received from Violet Purple, the Group audit engagement
partner.
2. Background information - information about the Magenta Group’s operations and activities.
3. Meeting notes - notes from a meeting between Violet Purple and the Group finance director.
4.
Email from Group audit committee - an email received by Violet Purple from the Chair of the
Group’s audit committee, Cyan Blue.
In this question, the partner’s email specified that Exhibits 2, 3 and 4 should be
considered when carrying out the requested risk evaluations. It is often the case that
there will be interactions between the exhibits which will impact on the analysis
performed by candidates. For example, in this question, the external focus on the
Group’s new renewables division described in Exhibit 2 was also relevant to
discussing the need for the Group to report it as an operating segment, which was
detailed further in Exhibit 3. By connecting the external pressure from stakeholders
and the significant increase in revenue from renewables in the current year, allowed
candidates to better discuss the risk of possible management bias or overstatement
of revenue being disclosed in this area of the Group.
As always, candidates are encouraged to spend adequate time reading and planning
and should aim to obtain a holistic view and understanding of the issues present in
the question.
Exhibit 1 – Partner’s email
In a Section A question, the partner’s email will always set out the detailed
requirements which are to be answered and the mark allocation. It is recommended
that candidates refer to the partner’s email first to ensure that they understand
what they are being asked to do as they read through the detailed information and to
help them consider the best way to allocate their time to each requirement.
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  4
The requirements of this question were split into four parts. Requirement (a) of the
question asked candidates to evaluate the significant business risks facing the
Group. Requirement (b) asked candidates to justify why specific areas had been
identified as significant risks of material misstatement (RoMM) relevant to the
information provided. Requirement (c) asked candidates to design the principal audit
procedures to be performed in respect of the impairment on the Group’s oil and gas
extraction platforms. Finally, requirement (d) asked candidates to discuss the
auditor's responsibilities and resultant impact on planning arising from a suspicious
payment.
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  5
Professional skills marks were available for all four of the professional skills
associated with the syllabus.
General comments
Well prepared candidates scored good marks on this question, particularly when
focusing on the specific scenario. Where a candidate prepared an answer tailored to
the scenario and focused on the requirement, high technical and professional skills
marks were obtained.
Some candidates continued to produce very vague answers which were not tailored
to the scenario and, therefore, did not achieve high technical or professional skills
marks. This exam requires candidates to demonstrate a high level of technical
knowledge, but they need to be able to apply this knowledge to a specific context.
Generic responses which do not build on the detail provided in the scenario will gain
little credit. Candidates should use the specific information provided within the
scenario to demonstrate both knowledge and application of skills to pass each
requirement.
Requirement (a) – 10 marks
(a) Evaluate the significant business risks to be considered in planning the
Group audit.
(10 marks)
In this section of the question, candidates were required to use the specific
information provided in the scenario to evaluate the business risks in relation to the
Group. In the AAA exam and in line with ISA 315 (Revised) Identifying and
Assessing the Risks of Material Misstatement, an evaluation of significant business
risks should include those risks which would have a significant impact on the client
business and where there is a significant probability of these risks occurring, after
any mitigations stated in the information provided. Risks that are of a remote
likelihood of occurring, already mitigated against or will have an insignificant impact
are not considered relevant to the evaluation. Candidates are required to identify
what is significant in the context of the specific scenario, demonstrating good
professional judgement and an ability to disseminate the important information while
assessing the risks.
This requirement was generally well answered, with the majority of candidates able
to identify and evaluate the risks surrounding the Group’s dependency on being able
to find viable new sources of oil and gas for extraction, how highly regulated the oil
and gas industry is and the significant impact of any breaches of that regulation, and
the reputation damage and significant costs which would arise from any accidents or
oil spills. Most answers identified the risk associated with the decommissioning of oil
platforms and the significant costs involved, but only the best answers were able to
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  6
discuss the uncertainty attached to these costs due to the extensive timeframes
involved. Stronger answers were also able to explore in detail the impact of the
decreasing demand for fossil fuels and how a sustained decrease in the value of oil
would make the Group’s operations prohibitively expensive to run.
In some answers the risk was not always well explained, which restricts the marks
which can be awarded. Many candidates only scratched the surface of the
evaluation by stating for example, that the Group is reliant on operating licences from
governments across the world, without explaining exactly how this might manifest in
a business risk for the Group. For example, stating that the Group needs these
licences to operate is only part of the risk. It is important to also clearly state the
implications to the Group if these licences were revoked or not renewed and the
circumstances under which that might happen. Many companies are required to hold
licences of this nature and it is just a necessary cost of operating in a certain
industry. The risk arises when there is a chance that the licence is no longer
available. In the case of the Magenta Group, that might arise because of
environmental pressure or a failure to meet the strict conditions. For the Magenta
Group, this could also result in a very significant amount of assets being impaired or
costs having to be expensed. Candidates must ensure they fully evaluate the risk
and its impact in order to maximise their technical and professional skills marks.
Overall, in this section many candidates were able to evaluate sufficient risks to pass
the requirement and the majority of candidates did not seem to have difficulty in
identifying business risks and the implication on the Group; however the
differentiator between stronger and weaker candidates was their ability to evaluate
the risks and demonstrate their professional skills of commercial acumen and
professional scepticism.
Requirement (b) – 14 marks
(b) Justify why each of the following issues has been identified as a
significant risk of material misstatement:
(i) Operating licences;
(ii) Operating segments;
(iii) Oil and gas extraction platforms.
You will need to determine and apply an appropriate materiality threshold
in your briefing notes.
(14 marks)
This requirement is typical in volume and nature to many planning questions and
examines a major area of the syllabus – risk of material misstatement.  In this
question, the requirement incorporated the format where candidates are asked to
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  7
focus their answer on specific areas and justify why they are considered high risk.
Although the wording of this requirement is more specific the skill is the same,
candidates are expected to perform relevant analysis to support an evaluation of
these specific risks of material misstatement (RoMMs). There were 14 technical
marks available in this part of the question in addition to a significant number of
professional skills marks for the analysis. It was disappointing to see that some
candidates obtained few marks for this requirement as they continue to rely on basic
or generic explanations, which fail to refer to the information in the scenario.
Candidates who refer to the specific information provide more in-depth answers and
are also able to assess the scale of the risk in the context of the specific audit client.
Materiality
Specific marks were available in this requirement for the calculation and application
of materiality in line with the syllabus guidance. While a significant number of
candidates appeared prepared and followed the materiality guidance provided by the
examining team, a significant number failed to apply this guidance and lost out on
valuable technical and professional skills marks in this area.
Candidates are expected to initially determine a materiality threshold for the audit, as
would be used in practice. Three technical marks are available for the materiality
determination. Candidates are expected to demonstrate a knowledge of the
appropriate percentage range for the benchmark as instructed by the audit
engagement partner (in this question, profit before tax was to be utilised with
candidates expected to use 5 - 10% as their range) and calculate the monetary
amount in respect of the range. Candidates must then use their professional
judgement to select an appropriate materiality threshold given the risk levels which
exist in the audit and provide a brief justification for their choice. Each of these steps
examines a different aspect of the understanding and skills required of an auditor.
Generally, materiality was not well applied. Many candidates ignored the guidance in
the partner’s email and used a different benchmark or produced the wrong
calculations. Some candidates wasted time by doing all the calculations correctly but
then failed to apply their own materiality threshold throughout their answer and
reverted to doing calculations for each item based on total assets or profit before tax.
This resulted in many candidates wasting time and also failing to score the
professional skills marks attached to this area.
It was also disappointing to note that some candidates failed to justify a materiality
threshold for the audit. The examining team will give credit for any reasonable
explanation of the chosen materiality threshold, as the mark is to reward candidates
who can justify their response through the application of professional judgement. It is
not required that candidates select the identical percentage or figure, or that they
provide a justification identical to that shown in the model answer. However, simply
stating that ‘materiality has been set at a specific value due to the risks which have
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  8
been identified’ is not considered a suitable explanation by the examining team and
will not be awarded credit. Many candidates used this as their explanation and
therefore were not able to score the full technical marks in this area. It was also
disappointing to note that some candidates do not understand the relationship
between audit risk and materiality. Some candidates concluded that as there was a
high level of risk present within the audit the materiality level could be increased,
thus failing to understand that there is an inverse relationship between audit risk and
the materiality level which should be set. Understanding this relationship is assumed
knowledge and candidates are encouraged to revisit the principles of materiality
which were explored in Audit and Assurance (AA) at the Applied Skills level.
Evaluation of the risks
Candidates were required to justify why operating licences, operating segments and
the Group’s oil and gas extraction platforms had been identified as significant
RoMMs. In any effective risk evaluation, candidates need to demonstrate an
understanding of risk, how it arises and how the audit will focus on those issues most
likely to cause a material misstatement. Candidates who demonstrated a depth of
evaluation were awarded more credit than those providing more generic responses.
Disappointingly, many candidates continue to provide answers where the risks are
not well explained and lack depth.
The majority of candidates were able to effectively evaluate the RoMMs associated
with the Group’s operating licences. Many candidates demonstrated appropriate
knowledge of IAS 38 Intangible Assets and were able to apply this to the question
and challenge the appropriateness of the Group’s amortisation policy. Most
candidates recognised that the average life used by the Group would result in some
licences being amortised over too long a timeframe, resulting in overstated intangible
assets and understated amortisation costs. Better answers were able to justify this
as a RoMM due to the significance of the carrying amount of the licences and the
estimation uncertainty which arose as a result of this amortisation policy. These
candidates were awarded the professional skills marks which were available for this
aspect of the requirement. Only the best answers made the connection between the
decrease in the demand for the Group’s products and the possible impairment of the
licences, as a reduction in the demand for fossil fuels may result in the early closure
of some of the extraction platforms for which the licences had been granted.
The risks arising from the Group’s operating segments was less well tackled. A good
number of candidates demonstrated knowledge of the requirements of IFRS 8
Operating Segments and what constitutes an operating segment but did not always
then recognise that there was a risk of inappropriate or incomplete disclosure in the
Group’s financial statements because of the new renewables division. Stronger
answers were able to use the scenario effectively to evaluate the risk of
management bias around the Group’s new renewables division and discuss how as
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  9
result of investor pressure, management may be motivated to overstate the revenue
disclosed in this new operating segment. This was also an effective basis for
candidates to justify why this area was identified as a RoMM. Candidates who were
able to discuss the difficulties in assessing the disclosure due to the fact that this
operating segment was not reported in the prior year and therefore no comparative
information was available showed excellent levels of judgement and application and
maximised their marks in this area.
Answers in relation to the risks arising from the Group’s oil and gas extraction
platforms were generally poor and many candidates either did not attempt this area
or provided very vague and short answers. As mentioned previously, candidates are
not expected to have detailed industry knowledge and therefore candidates who
were able to provide good answers in this area used strong exam technique and
worked through the detailed information in Exhibit 3. The oil and gas extraction
platforms resulted in significant RoMMs and marks were available for assessing how
these assets were depreciated, any necessary impairment and the costs of
decommissioning the platforms at the end of their useful life. All these areas were
detailed in Exhibit 3. The mention of impairment in relation to these assets at
requirement (c) should also have suggested to candidates that impairment was a
significant risk area as procedures are often directed towards the high risk areas of
the audit.
The majority of candidates who provided answers in relation to the depreciation of
these assets scored marks for highlighting the increased inherent risk due to the
complexity of the calculations which involved significant management judgement and
therefore there was a risk of depreciation being incorrectly calculated.
Disappointingly though, only a small minority of candidates drew on their knowledge
of IAS 16 Property, Plant and Equipment to conclude that as the depreciation
method used should reflect the pattern in which an asset’s economic benefits are
consumed, it was likely that the Group’s policy of charging depreciation based on the
proportion of oil and gas extracted was an appropriate approach. Many candidates
just assumed that because the policy was not the normal straight line approach it
was incorrect.
Most answers which discussed the impairment of the platforms were able to draw on
the requirements of IAS 36 Impairment of Assets, around when an impairment
review should be conducted and that assets should be held at the lower of carrying
amount and recoverable amount. Many candidates also discussed the risks arising
because of the complexity in determining the value in use for the oil platforms and
that the Group’s use of a single discount rate for all impairment calculations was
likely to be too optimistic given the range of environments in which the Group
operated. Strong answers went on to discuss the requirements of IAS 36 in relation
to the reversal of impairment losses and gained professional skills marks for showing
appropriate levels of professional scepticism.
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  10
Again many candidates struggled to come up with appropriate risks around the
decommissioning costs in relation to the oil platforms and much of this was due to a
lack of accounting knowledge. Many candidates stated that it was inappropriate to
include the costs of decommissioning as part of the asset costs and wasted time
focussing on the risk that costs were overstated as a result. Instead candidates
needed to focus again on the complexity of deriving those costs, especially due to
the timescales involved, the difficulty in determining an appropriate discount factor
and the need for estimates to be reviewed and updated regularly. It was more likely
that management would understate the provision associated with the platforms and
therefore also that the cost of the assets would be understated.
Requirement (c) – 8 marks
(c) Design the principal audit procedures to be performed on the
impairment of the cash generating units for the extraction of oil and gas.
(8 marks)
This requirement is typical of a section A question and requires candidates to design
audit procedures to address a specific area of risk arising in the question. In this
question, candidates were required to design the principal audit procedures in
respect of the impairment review which had been conducted in relation to the
Group’s oil and gas extraction assets (oil and gas platforms) which was described in
detail in Exhibit 3. Candidates were told that each platform was treated as a separate
cash generating unit (CGU) by the Group. Overall, responses to requirement (c)
were mixed. Many candidates were unable to convert their accounting knowledge in
relation to the impairment of assets into appropriate audit procedures.
The weakest answers focussed on reviewing board minutes for a discussion of the
impairment review performed by management, a physical inspection of the oil and
gas platforms or to agree the carrying amount of the CGU to the asset register.
Some candidates also wanted to agree the cost of the assets to invoice which
showed a lack of understanding in relation to the type of asset detailed in the
scenario. None of these procedures would be effective in auditing the completeness
or effectiveness of the impairment review and therefore scored no credit.
Better answers used the information provided in the scenario as the basis for the
procedures, for example by designing procedures to challenge management’s
assumption around the difficulty in obtaining a market value for the assets or
obtaining copies of the value in use calculations prepared by management and
challenging/verifying the assumptions used. Strong answers also went on to test
whether the discount rate used by management was appropriate or the basis for the
impairment reversals recorded in the year.
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  11
The strongest candidates gained a professional skills mark under analysis and
evaluation for designing procedures which addressed the areas of judgment and risk
in verifying the impairment which was necessary on the oil and gas CGUs.
Requirement (d) – 8 marks
In relation to the matters arising from the email from the Chair of the audit
committee
(d) (i) Discuss the auditor’s responsibilities; and
(ii) Describe the impact on planning the audit.
(8 marks)
Requirement (d) asked candidates to discuss the responsibilities of the auditor in
relation to a suspicious payment which had been identified. Exhibit 4 detailed an
email received from the audit committee which highlighted a payment which had
been uncovered by the internal audit department as lying outside the Group’s normal
internal controls process. The payment appeared to have been made to help the
Group in secure a new operating licence. Candidates were also asked to consider
the impact that this issue might have on the audit planning, specifically as the audit
committee was concerned that this payment had not been detected by Plum & Co
during the interim audit. The Partner’s email directed candidates to use “the email
from the Chair of the audit committee (Exhibit 5)”. Although the name of the exhibit
was correct, it incorrectly referred to “Exhibit 5”, which did not exist. However, this
does not appear to have caused candidates any difficulty in identifying the right
information or attempting the requirement.
The responses to this requirement were mixed. Most candidates were able to
recognise this payment as a potential bribe or illegal payment and that this was likely
to constitute non-compliance with laws and regulations. Many candidates were also
able to discuss whether the auditor would have to report this to regulators outside of
the Group and to consider this in terms of confidentiality. Better answers were able
to explain the auditor’s responsibilities when non-compliance is identified or
suspected and that further procedures would now be required in order to understand
the nature of the payment and whether it was isolated.
As is often the case at AAA, weaker answers were able to identify the issues but not
specifically relate them to the circumstances of the Magenta Group. For example,
many candidates discussed how the incident now needed to be reported to
management or those charged with governance, which failed to take into account
that they are already aware, as it was the audit committee who brought it to the
auditor's attention. Stronger answers instead were able to suggest that management
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  12
and the audit committee needed to be encouraged to make any necessary
disclosures themselves to the relevant authorities.
Requirement (d)(ii) was handled less well, and many candidates failed to consider
the impact of this issue on the audit planning at all in their answer. Poorer answers
were able to identify that further evidence would be required to understand the
impact but did not give any detail about what that evidence would be or how it would
be gathered. Good answers were able to discuss the impact this issue might have on
the firm’s assessment of management integrity and what that might mean for audit
evidence in other areas.
The best answers also described how this issue might affect the firm’s assessment
of the Group’s internal controls and how that might modify the audit plan in terms of
controls testing vs substantive testing. Many of these candidates were also then able
to challenge the audit committee’s comment about why the external auditor had not
detected this issue as part of the interim audit work, including an assessment of the
materiality of the issue as well as the difficulty in detecting instances of management
override of controls. Candidates who provided a tailored, balanced and detailed
discussion in relation to this suspected non-compliance with laws and regulations
were also awarded the associated professional skills marks.
Overall professional skill marks
In addition to the professional skills marks described within the different sections of
the question, four marks were available for communication overall. These marks
were awarded for the use of a report header and introduction, presentation and
relevance of answer and clarity of explanations. The majority of candidates achieved
maximum marks in this area.
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  13
Section B

Question 2 – Pickle
Pickle & Co was a 25-mark question set around two clients, Brook Coal Co and the
Suki Group, and focussed on ethical issues which needed to be considered prior to
continuing or extending the client relationships.
Overall candidates performed better on requirement (a) as many were able to
evaluate a wider range of points in order to score marks. Requirement (b) was often
only a couple of short paragraphs or bullet points and some candidates failed to
consider the information in any depth, proving a list of generic comments about the
possible ethical threats. As such, these candidates were not able to demonstrate the
professional skills that were also examined in the question of professional scepticism
and judgement, analysis and evaluation and commercial acumen.
Candidates are again reminded to use published questions and marking guides to
aid them when planning answers and considering the number of detailed points
which need to be made for the marks available.
It is 1 July 20X5. You are a manager in Pickle & Co, a firm of Chartered Certified
Accountants which offers a range of assurance and accountancy services. As part
of your firm’s system of quality management, independence assessments are
required for all new client engagements and as part of the continuation process for
existing clients prior to reappointment.
There were two exhibits:
1.
Brook Coal Co – identified threats and proposed safeguards from the audit
manager’s continuation assessment.
2.
Suki Group – client billing information for an existing non-audit client inviting
Pickle & Co to tender for the audit.
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  14
Requirement (a) – 10 marks
Using the information in Exhibit 1:
(a) (i) Explain the ethical threats identified by the audit engagement
manager; and
(b) (ii) Evaluate the proposed safeguards and, where appropriate,
recommend alternative safeguards which may be applied by Pickle &
Co.
(10 marks)
Candidates were provided with details of the audit engagement for Brook Coal Co
including the composition of the audit team, the length of time the current team had
been involved with the audit as well as some other specific issues which would affect
this year’s audit, such as staff members who were leaving the firm and the client.
Candidates were also provided with the initial assessment of the ethical issues which
had been identified by the audit manager and the proposed safeguards to mitigate
for these threats.
Candidates were asked to explain the ethical threats which had been identified by
the audit manager and to evaluate the proposed safeguards, suggesting alternatives
where relevant. Responses to requirement a(i) and a(ii) were mixed. Candidates
were expected to take the high level identification of the threat which was given in
the question and provide a detailed explanation of why this presents an ethical issue.
For example, to explain why the audit team being in place for seven years presents
an ethical threat to the engagement. Most candidates were able to use this
information in the scenario to identify how this long association gives rise to a
possible familiarity threat and why this is a specific issue for the audit engagement
partner. Weaker answers often stopped at this point and therefore essentially
ignored the detail in the scenario. While the identification of the type of ethical threat
is relevant, and scored some technical credit, the marks are severely restricted
where candidates do not make these comments applied to the specifics of the client
they have been asked to consider. Therefore, many candidates failed to provide any
explanation as to the impact of this ethical issue on the audit.
Candidates are again reminded that there are minimal knowledge marks available in
AAA, and to maximise marks, candidates must be tailoring and applying that
knowledge to the specifics of the scenario they are presented with. As with any
requirement focussed on ethics, candidates need to be prepared to go further than
simply identifying the nature of the threat and must be able to fully explain the
impact of the issue. The ethics section of the AAA syllabus builds on the Audit and
Assurance syllabus and candidates must ensure that their assumed knowledge is
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  15
up-to-date and at the right level. Question practice is crucial to ensure that detailed
knowledge of the IESBA International Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants
(the Code) can be applied and used to answer the more complex questions which
will be presented in the AAA exam.
Brook Coal Co is a listed company and therefore better answers were able to score
marks for applying and discussing the rules included in the Code regarding the
length of time an individual can serve as audit engagement partner, including the
possibility of serving for an additional year to maintain audit quality. Only the best
answers were able to score further marks by appropriately applying this exception to
the details in the scenario. As the resignation of the current engagement quality
reviewer (EQR) and the finance director of Brook Coal Co could be used as a basis
for suggesting that to ensure continuity and quality the current audit partner should
be allowed to continue for an extra year.
Stronger answers also recognised that while there is no specific rule in the Code,
this familiarity threat may extend to the whole team and that the associated risk
would be higher for more senior members of the team, such as the audit manager,
who has more influence over the outcome of the audit. Strong answers went on to
discuss how the current resource within the office of the firm would affect the
safeguards which were available. Professional skills marks were awarded to
answers which subsequently discussed the lack of succession planning at Pickle &
Co given the lack of staff with experience in the relevant industry and that this
represented a failure in the firm’s system of quality management.
Many candidates struggled with the points related to the EQR. Many answers
showed a lack of knowledge of the requirements for listed entities to have an EQR in
place and therefore that the suggested safeguard to introduce this type of review
would do little to mitigate for the familiarity threat. Some candidates were able to
generically discuss the requirements included in ISQM 2 Engagement Quality
Reviews, around the competence and independence of the EQR. However, only the
best answers were able to use the scenario proficiently to evaluate the suggested
safeguard and challenge why the suggested EQR replacements would give rise to
additional ethical and professional threats for the engagement.
At this level candidates should be aiming to integrate the points in the scenario with
their answer points and looking for the various connections between the information
which is presented.
In relation to the last part of requirement (a) which asked candidates to recommend
alternative safeguards which might be more effective in the circumstances,
responses were also mixed. Some candidates simply listed generic safeguards
which were not appropriate in relation to Brook Coal Co, such as appoint an
engagement quality reviewer or a different audit engagement partner. Such
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  16
suggestions failed to recognise that given the current resources which were available
within the firm this was not immediately possible. Effective answers were able to
weigh up the ethical threats and the commercial information provided to suggest
suitable actions such as recruiting from the firm’s wider network to ensure that team
members had adequate skills and experience. Professional skills marks were
awarded where candidates demonstrated this commercial awareness and provided
effective challenge to the suggested safeguards by the audit manager.
Requirement (b) – 10 marks
Using the information in Exhibit 2:
(c) Evaluate the professional and ethical matters relating to fees and impact
of the proposed listing which should be considered by Pickle & Co prior
to accepting appointment as the auditor for the financial statements of
the Suki Group.
(10 marks)
Candidates were again provided with some detailed information about the Suki
Group, an existing client currently provided with other assurance services. The client
was looking to appoint a new auditor and had approached Pickle & Co to tender for
the audit. Candidates were given detailed information about the work currently
provided, as well as the level of fees charged for existing work and the proposed
audit fee. Candidates were asked to consider the ethical matters arising from the
proposed appointment as auditor to the Group.
This requirement was generally not well answered. Many candidates provided vague
and generic answers, failing to consider the overall mark allocation and the detailed
information which was provided. Some candidates showed a lack of knowledge of
the requirements of the Code in relation to fee levels and the difference in guidance
between listed and unlisted entities. In this scenario the Group was currently unlisted
but was rapidly expanding and was planning to seek a listing in the next two years.
Therefore, the best answers were able to consider the ethical issues from both
angles and what the firm needed to consider both currently, and in the future, if the
Group obtained listed status. This was specifically asked for in the requirement and it
was disappointing to see that many candidates did not consider the impact of the
Group obtaining a listing as part of their evaluation. Candidates are once again
reminded to read the requirement carefully to ensure that they are addressing all
aspects of the question.
Strong answers understood that there was a risk of fee dependency and used the
fee information in the scenario to provide meaningful analysis and calculations to
support their discussion around whether Pickle & Co should consider tendering for
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  17
the audit and were awarded professional skills marks accordingly. Most candidates
were able to suggest some appropriate actions in relation to this issue, such as the
firm could reduce the level of other services offered if they wanted to become auditor
in order to reduce the firm’s dependency on the Group.
It was disappointing to see that few candidates were able to discuss the issues
related to the proposed discount offered on the audit fee in depth. While many
identified that a self-interest threat may arise, many were unable to discuss the
inappropriate basis of the discount and the impact this might have on the firm’s
independence and objectivity.

Question 3 – Cuckoo
This 25-mark question was set at the completion and reporting stage of the audit of
the Cuckoo Group, a listed manufacturer.  As is typical of reporting questions, the
examining team saw some of the strongest and some of the weakest demonstrations
of auditing competence from candidates in this question.
This question presented candidates with details about issues which had been
highlighted for the audit manager’s attention in relation to the Group and one of its
subsidiaries, Landry Co. The majority of marks available in the question were for
commenting on the matters to be considered in relation to the highlighted issues and
the evidence which should be included in the audit working papers. This is a
common style of question in the AAA exam and candidates appeared comfortable
with the familiar format of the question and the answers provided were appropriately
structured.
Professional skills marks were available for analysis and evaluation, professional
scepticism and judgement and commercial acumen.
It is 1 July 20X5. You are an audit manager in Strike & Co, responsible for the
audit of the Cuckoo Group (the Group) for the year ended 31 March 20X5. All
companies within the Group have the same financial year end. The auditor’s report
is due to be issued in two weeks’ time.
The Group is listed and operates in the manufacturing industry. The draft
consolidated financial statements recognise revenue of $810 million (20X4: $732
million), profit before tax of $34 million (20X4: $24 million) and total assets of $516
million (20X4: $504 million). Group materiality has been set at $5 million in line
with Strike & Co's internal quality procedures.
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  18
Requirement (a) – 5 marks – International and Singapore Exams
(a) Discuss the Group auditor’s considerations when determining which
components Group audit work should be performed on.
Note: You do not need to us an exhibit when answering this requirement.
(5 marks)
Requirement (a) was a standalone requirement which asked candidates to discuss
what the Group auditor should consider when determining which components audit
work should be performed on. Answers to this requirement were generally poor.
The requirement needed understanding of ISA 600 (Revised) Specials
Considerations – Audits of Group Financial Statements (Including the Work for
Component Auditors) and the matters which might influence the Group auditor's
determination of the level of work needed on each component. This is a revision to
ISA 600, and it was disappointing to note that many students had failed to
understand the changes set out in the revised standard or take note of the
information included in the technical article available on ACCA’s website.
Only a minority of candidates were able to discuss that determining the components
at which to perform audit work is a matter of significant auditor judgement. Few
candidates demonstrated an understanding that certain activities, such as the
acquisition of a subsidiary or significant related party transactions, would form part of
the Group auditor’s consideration in this regard. Only the best answers
demonstrated an understanding that much of these considerations would be as a
direct result of the assessment of the risk of material misstatement present within the
component, which may be driven by issues such as previous control issues or
inconsistencies within analytical procedures.
Unfortunately, a large number of candidates confused the term component (ie a
subsidiary or entity of the Group) with the term component auditor and therefore
provided an answer which was focussed on determining the amount of reliance
which could placed on other audit firms. While this might be part of the determination
many candidates were only able to score minimal marks as this did not fully answer
the requirement set.
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  19
Requirement (a) – 5 marks - UK and Ireland Adapted Exams
The Corporate Governance Code requires audit committees to assess the work and
effectiveness of the external auditor.
(a) Discuss the main areas of audit quality which should be evaluated.
Note: You do not need to use an exhibit when answering this requirement.
(5 marks)
Requirement (a) in the UK and Ireland adapted versions of the exam was different.
This was due to a difference in the publication dates of ISA 600 (Revised) and
therefore a delay in its inclusion on the list of examinable documents for the UK and
IRL versions of the exam. As such, candidates sitting these versions of the exam
were asked to consider how the audit committee should assess the work of the
external auditor in order to maintain audit quality.
Overall responses were disappointing. Many candidates were able to identify that the
audit committee should ensure that the external auditor is independent and has the
appropriate knowledge and experience to carry out the work to a high standard.
However, only the best answers were able to discuss the importance of the firm’s
system of quality management and how that supports the audit team in applying
professional scepticism and challenging management and in ensuring that the audit
methodology and resources are appropriately tailored to the engagement. Very few
candidates mentioned the importance of the auditor's judgement and that the audit
committee will want to ensure that this is exercised appropriately at all stages of the
audit.
Requirement (b) – 11 marks
Using the information provided in Exhibit 1:
(b) Comment on the matters to be considered in relation to each of the
issues identified below and explain the audit evidence you should
expect to find during your review of the Group audit working papers:
(i) Possible classification of Landry Co as held for sale;
(ii) Possible classification of Landry Co as a discontinued operation and
any impact on the Group financial statements.
(11 marks)
Candidates were generally able to discuss the criteria which need to be satisfied for
assets to be classified as held for sale as set out in IFRS 5 Non-current Assets Held
for Sale and Discontinued Operations. Stronger answers were then also able to use
the information in the scenario to demonstrate how the criteria may have been met.
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  20
For example, by discussing that the presence of potential buyers suggested that
there was an active programme to locate a buyer, and that the advanced stage of
negotiations and due diligence being conducted suggested that Landry Co was
available for immediate sale and that a sale was highly probable.
Fewer candidates were clear on the criteria set out in IFRS 5 regarding the definition
of a discontinued operation. Only better answers were able to discuss that Landry
Co was the only foreign subsidiary held by the Group and therefore was likely to
represent a separate major geographical area and if It was classified as held for
sale, it was also likely to be a discontinued operation.
A good number of candidates were able to score technical marks for discussing the
necessary accounting treatment if Landry Co was to be classified as held for sale,
including that the assets should be recognised as current assets, that depreciation
would no longer be charged and that the assets should be reviewed for impairment.
However, only stronger answers were able to use the information in the scenario to
suggest that it was unlikely that Landry Co was impaired by comparing the carrying
amount with the proposed sales price. These candidates often demonstrated
effective professional scepticism and were awarded professional skills marks by
understanding that the achievability of the sales prices would need to be confirmed
via appropriate audit evidence.
Many candidates were also able to recognise that there was a likely material
misstatement in the Group financial statements if Landry Co was not classified as
held for sale / disclosed as a discontinued operation. Better answers were able to
take this a stage further and discuss the impact this lack of disclosure would have on
the users understanding of the financial statements. Professional skills marks were
awarded where candidates where able to effectively challenge the accounting
treatment and approach adopted by management.
Answers in relation to the evidence which should be included in the audit working
papers were generally satisfactory. Many candidates were able to suggest that
evidence was needed over the marketing programme which had been put in place.
Also that the negotiations with the prospective buyers including possible draft
contracts, and how the sale price had been determined which included the
assessment of an expert valuation. Weaker answers focussed solely on obtaining
items such as board minutes for the approval of the sale or obtaining a written
representation from management, neither of which are sufficient evidence to deal
with the key issues present in the scenario. Stronger answers were able to also
suggest evidence in relation to the impairment review which should have been
conducted by management or subsequent events procedures which looked at
whether any further progress had been made on the sale. Candidates were awarded
professional skills marks where the evidence provided tackled the main risks and
issues coming from the scenario.
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  21
Requirement (c) – 4 marks
It is now 1 September 20X5 and the auditor’s report is due to be signed next week.
The audit team has provided you with the following completion update:
Completion update
The Group has selected a buyer and is at the advanced stage of negotiations with
the sale expected to occur by the end of October 20X5. Following discussion with
the Group finance director, the consolidated financial statements have not been
amended with disclosure of the planned disposal of Landry Co.
(c) Using the information provided in the completion update, justify an
appropriate audit opinion and explain the impact on the auditor’s report.
(4 marks)
Requirement (c) then moved the candidates on in time and advised that the sale of
Landry Co was now at an advanced stage and was expected to occur by the end of
October 20X5. Candidates were asked to justify an appropriate audit opinion and
explain the impact on the auditor’s report where management had made no
adjustments to the disclosure in the financial statements as a result of this planned
disposal. Answers in relation to auditor reporting and audit opinions continue to be
generally unsatisfactory.
It continues to be disappointing to see that many candidates do not present a single
audit opinion and often include an answer which covers multiple outcomes. In this
instance, many candidates provided an opinion for a variety of outcomes: if the
disclosures were made (even though they have been told that they are not included);
if the misstatements were material but not pervasive; and if the misstatements were
material and pervasive. Candidates are once again reminded that they should focus
on what is relevant in the circumstances as this can result in valuable minutes being
wasted on points which do not score credit. This type of approach is not effective as
it means that many candidates waste time providing inappropriate analysis and it
also affects their ability to score the professional skills marks in this area as they fail
to select and justify the appropriate audit opinion.
Many candidates were able to conclude that the lack of disclosure in relation to
Landry Co would result in a modification because of the material misstatements in
the financial statements. Disappointingly, a high number of candidates were not able
to provide meaningful analysis on the pervasiveness of the issue. Stronger answers
were able to demonstrate that due to the nature of the issue, it was likely that the
impact was pervasive as it would affect nearly every area of the financial statements,
and therefore an adverse opinion would be appropriate. Candidates are again
reminded to ensure that they have a good knowledge of auditor reporting
requirements and understanding of the variety of modifications which are available
Examiner’s report – AAA March/June 2024  22
and the circumstances under which they apply. Answers were awarded professional
skills marks where detailed analysis supported conclusions provided.
Candidates should ensure that they review the model answer to understand the level
of depth and analysis which is needed to score the technical and professional skills
marks in relation to auditor reporting.
Conclusion
The performance of candidates in these questions is broadly in line with past
sessions. There continues to be a gap between candidates capable of demonstrating
audit competence through strong application of knowledge and concepts to practical
scenarios, and those who approach the examination as a factual exercise and fail to
tailor their answers to the scenarios or do not show professional scepticism or
commercial awareness. Many candidates also continue to show a lack of what
should be assumed knowledge for materiality, ethics and auditor reporting and
continue to produce answers of a standard below what is expected at AAA level.
Practice of past questions will aid candidates in determining their knowledge gaps
and give practice at applying their knowledge to the given scenarios. It is essential
that candidates analyse their answers in comparison to the scenario specific
explanations given in the model answers to understand the level of analysis which is
needed in the AAA exam. This question practice needs to also be based on a good
knowledge of the syllabus and must build upon both the Applied Skills level audit and
financial reporting examinations and the Strategic Business Reporting (SBR)
syllabus. Candidates are encouraged to ensure that their assumed knowledge in
both of these areas is at an appropriate level before commencing their studies
towards the AAA exam.
Candidates are encouraged to develop a wider appreciation of the significance of
risks by reviewing published auditor’s reports of listed companies where the auditor
produces a key audit matters section. This will allow candidates to see real world
explanations by auditors of why something was a specific risk to a specific client.
Information and guidance on the introduction of professional skills marks for AAA is
available as part of the study resources on the ACCA website.
